title: Wire Reduction: 6→3 Signal Multiplexing for HF55 Shaft

problem.summary: The Melexis thumbwheel in the ThumbJoy grip needs 6 wires routed through the HF55 hollow shaft, but the shaft is too tight. The HF55 already has 3 wires going through its shaft for the original buttons (which are NOT used by ThumbJoy). If we can multiplex the 6 Melexis signals down to 3 or fewer wires, we can reuse the existing shaft wire path.
problem.existingPath: HF55 has 3 wires already routed through shaft (for original buttons, currently unused)
constraints: Circuit must physically fit inside the top grip housing next to the Melexis sensor
Must be extremely small — grip housing is tight (custom Melexis bracket screws into handle bosses)
Power available: VCC and GND come up through the shaft (2 of the 3 existing wires)
Only 1 remaining wire for data (or repurpose all 3 for VCC + GND + DATA)
Must not interfere with Melexis magnet or Z-axis Hall sensing
Teensy 4.1 at the bottom can support SPI, I2C, UART, or analog input

melexisInfo.currentSensor: Melexis Hall effect sensor (specific model TBD — likely MLX90316 or MLX90333)
melexisInfo.currentOutput: Likely dual analog (2 analog outputs for thumbwheel position), which requires: VCC, GND, OUT1, OUT2, and possibly 2 more for a second sensor = 6 wires total
melexisInfo.spiCapability: Both MLX90316 and MLX90333 natively support a 3-wire SPI protocol (/SS, SCLK, MOSI-MISO combined). This means the sensor ITSELF can output digitally on just 3 signal wires.
melexisInfo.keyInsight: If the Melexis sensor can be configured for SPI output instead of analog, the entire 6-wire problem may be solvable at the sensor level — no additional MCU needed.

approaches.name: Option A: Use Melexis SPI Mode Directly
approaches.wireBreakdown: VCC + GND + /SS + SCLK + MOSI/MISO (combined) = 5 wires total
approaches.description: Both MLX90316 and MLX90333 have a built-in 3-wire SPI serial protocol mode. Instead of using analog outputs (which need separate wires per axis), configure the Melexis for SPI output. The SPI protocol uses /SS (chip select), SCLK (clock), and a combined MOSI-MISO line — that's 3 signal wires + VCC + GND = 5 total.
pros: ZERO additional hardware — just a configuration change on the existing Melexis sensor
Melexis natively supports this (documented in datasheet)
SPI is well-supported by Teensy 4.1 (multiple SPI buses available)
Higher resolution digital data vs. analog (12-bit typical)
No EMI/noise issues from analog wire runs through magnetically active zone
No additional power consumption
cons: Still 5 wires (3 signal + VCC + GND) — may still be too many for shaft
Requires reprogramming Melexis via PTC-04 programmer (Melexis programming tool)
Alec Korver would need to update the ThumbJoy PCB firmware to read SPI instead of analog
If using dual Melexis (ThumbThumb version), need 2 separate /SS lines = 6 wires again
approaches.feasibility: HIGH — but only saves 1 wire (6→5). May not be enough.
nextSteps: Confirm exact Melexis model used in current ThumbJoy
Check if Melexis can share SCLK and MOSI/MISO lines (SPI bus) with separate /SS for dual sensors
Ask Alec Korver about Melexis programming capability

approaches.name: Option B: Tiny MCU in Grip — ADC + Serial Bus (BEST OPTION)
approaches.wireBreakdown: VCC + GND + DATA = 3 wires (reuse existing HF55 shaft wiring exactly)
approaches.description: Place a tiny microcontroller (ATtiny85, ATtiny412, or similar) inside the grip housing next to the Melexis sensor. The MCU reads the Melexis analog outputs locally, digitizes them via its built-in ADC, and sends the data down a single-wire serial bus (UART, I2C, or one-wire protocol) to the Teensy 4.1 in the base. Total wiring through shaft: VCC + GND + DATA = exactly 3 wires.

components.role: MCU — reads Melexis analog, transmits digital serial
components.size: 8-pin SOIC = 5mm x 4mm (ATtiny85) or even smaller QFN
components.notes: ATtiny85: 8 pins, 4 ADC channels, USI for I2C/SPI, UART via software. ATtiny412: even smaller, native UART, 3 ADC. Both run at 3.3V or 5V.

components.part: Pull-up resistor (4.7kΩ for I2C) or none for UART

circuitDesign.dataRate: Thumbwheel position needs ~100Hz update rate max. I2C at 100kHz or UART at 9600 baud is MORE than enough.
circuitDesign.powerDraw: ATtiny85 at 8MHz: ~5mA. Negligible vs. system budget.

firmwareSketch.gripMCU: ATtiny85 reads 2 analog Melexis outputs via ADC every 10ms, packs into 2-byte message, sends via I2C (as slave) or UART TX. Total code: ~50 lines of Arduino.
firmwareSketch.teensySide: Teensy reads I2C slave or UART RX. Gets 2 bytes = thumbwheel position. Replace existing analogRead() with I2C/UART read. ~10 lines of code change.

pros: EXACTLY 3 wires through shaft — perfect reuse of existing HF55 wiring path
Tiny board (< 8mm x 5mm) fits next to Melexis in existing grip housing
BOM cost ~$2
ATtiny85 is extremely well-documented (Arduino compatible)
No changes needed to the Melexis sensor itself — still uses analog output
Digital transmission = immune to EMI/noise through magnetically active shaft zone
Can easily extend to read additional sensors (buttons, kill switch) if needed
Alec Korver could design this into the next ThumbJoy PCB revision as an option
cons: Adds a component to maintain (firmware on ATtiny)
Needs programming tool (USBasp or Arduino as ISP) for ATtiny
Small latency addition (~1-2ms) — negligible for throttle control
Requires small custom PCB (or dead-bug soldering for prototype)
approaches.feasibility: VERY HIGH — proven technique, trivial firmware, tiny footprint. This is the recommended approach.
nextSteps: Confirm exact Melexis output pin count and voltage levels
Prototype on breadboard: ATtiny85 + 2 potentiometers (simulating Melexis) → I2C → Teensy 4.1
Test fit inside grip housing alongside Melexis bracket

approaches.name: Option C: Analog Multiplexer Chip (No MCU)
approaches.wireBreakdown: VCC + GND + Analog MUX output + Select line = 4 wires
approaches.description: Use an analog multiplexer IC (CD4051, CD4052, or 74HC4052) to time-share multiple analog signals over a single wire. The Teensy toggles a SELECT line, and the MUX switches between Melexis output 1 and output 2. Both signals share one analog wire.
pros: No firmware needed in the grip — purely analog solution
Very simple circuit (1 IC + 2 caps)
CD4051/4052 are dirt cheap ($0.20) and widely available
Teensy controls the switching — deterministic timing
cons: Still needs 4 wires (VCC + GND + Analog + Select) — one more than target
Analog signal still susceptible to EMI/noise through shaft
Multiplexing halves the effective sample rate per channel
SELECT line timing adds complexity to Teensy firmware
approaches.feasibility: MEDIUM — works but 4 wires may still be too tight in shaft. Option B is better.
nextSteps: Only pursue if Option B (MCU) is rejected for complexity reasons

approaches.name: Option D: Replace Melexis with Digital-Output Sensor
approaches.wireBreakdown: VCC + GND + I2C_SDA (SCL can be shared with SDA using SMBus) = 3 wires, or VCC + GND + SDA + SCL = 4 wires
approaches.description: Replace the current analog Melexis sensor with a digital-output variant (MLX90363 or MLX90393) that communicates via I2C or SPI natively. This eliminates the need for an intermediary MCU — the sensor talks directly to the Teensy via a digital bus running through the shaft.

sensorOptions.notes: High-speed SPI, programmable via Melexis PTC-04. 3D joystick mode available.
I2C is 2-wire data. Breakout boards available from SparkFun/Adafruit for prototyping. 3D magnetometer.
12-bit rotary position sensor. Very popular in DIY. Tiny SOIC-8 package. I2C output. Could replace Melexis thumbwheel sensor entirely.

pros: Cleanest long-term solution — sensor IS the digitizer
No additional MCU board needed
I2C sensors need only 4 wires total (some can share bus)
MLX90393 breakout boards available for instant prototyping
AS5600 is extremely popular with massive community support
cons: Requires changing the sensor, which means new Melexis bracket and PCB modifications
Alec Korver's double Melexis PCB would need redesign
Still 4 wires for I2C (unless using one-wire variant)
MLX90363 needs 5 wires (SPI)
approaches.feasibility: MEDIUM — clean but requires more hardware changes. Option B is faster to prototype.
nextSteps: Order MLX90393 or AS5600 breakout board for benchtop testing
Test I2C read from Teensy 4.1 through 30cm wire (simulating shaft length)

approaches.name: Option E: Single-Wire Protocol (Dallas 1-Wire / UART)
approaches.wireBreakdown: Power (parasitic from data line) + DATA = 2 wires, or VCC + DATA + GND = 3
approaches.description: Use a single-wire protocol to send data. ATtiny in grip reads Melexis, transmits via software UART on one pin. Even more extreme: use Dallas 1-Wire protocol with parasitic power — needs only 2 physical wires (GND + DATA, power derived from data line). Teensy reads via OneWire library.
pros: Absolute minimum wire count — 2 or 3 wires
Parasitic power mode needs only GND + DATA
OneWire is well-supported on Arduino/Teensy
cons: Parasitic power is unreliable for MCU (designed for sensors, not MCU)
Single-wire protocols are slower — fine for 100Hz thumbwheel but marginal
More complex firmware than I2C
Debugging single-wire protocols is painful
approaches.feasibility: LOW-MEDIUM — technically possible but over-engineering for this use case. Option B (I2C) is simpler and only needs 3 wires.
nextSteps: Only pursue if shaft can't fit 3 wires

recommendation.bestOption: Option B: Tiny MCU in Grip (ATtiny85 + I2C)
recommendation.reason: Exactly 3 wires (VCC + GND + SDA). Reuses existing HF55 shaft wiring. Board smaller than a fingernail (~8mm x 5mm). BOM ~$2. Trivial Arduino firmware. No changes to the Melexis sensor. No changes to the grip mechanics. Teensy firmware change is ~10 lines. Prototype possible in one afternoon with breadboard + ATtiny85 dev board.

parts: Sparkfun Tiny AVR Programmer or Arduino Uno as ISP
steps: 1. Wire ATtiny85 on breadboard with 2 pots on ADC pins
2. Flash ATtiny85 with I2C slave firmware (reads 2 ADC channels, responds to master)
3. Connect ATtiny85 I2C (SDA pin) to Teensy 4.1 I2C bus (SDA/SCL + GND)
4. Write Teensy sketch to poll ATtiny85 every 10ms for 2-byte thumbwheel position
5. Verify latency < 5ms and position accuracy matches direct analog read
6. If works: design tiny PCB (8mm x 5mm), order from JLCPCB
7. Test fit PCB inside grip housing next to Melexis bracket
quickPrototype.timeEstimate: 1-2 hours for breadboard prototype, 1 week for PCB

recommendation.alternativeIfI2CFailsDueToEMI: Use UART (software serial) instead of I2C. Single TX wire from ATtiny to Teensy RX. Still 3 wires total. UART is more noise-tolerant than I2C over short runs.
